demos.evidence_text: She reports three nocturnal clusters this month; each ~5 or 6 events
demos.note_text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Sophie Williams, DOB: 24-11-1991, Hospital No: N483720 NHS No. 9054412276  
12 Fernbank Close, London, W10 6QL 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. Lamotrigine 100 mg morning / 150 mg night. Midazolam 5 mg buccal PRN for prolonged clusters (not used this month). 

I reviewed Sophie Williams in neurology clinic with collateral information from employer occupational health, who provide routine summaries aligned to her wellness checks. She has Combined Generalised and Focal Epilepsy Type, with a history of both generalised tonic–clonic seizures and focal aware/aware-to-impaired awareness events. She reports three nocturnal clusters this month; each ~5 or 6 events, witnessed by her partner and corroborated by occupational health logs noting next‑day fatigue and headache. These predominantly occur between 02:00–04:00, start with a brief epigastric rising sensation and left hand tingling (suggestive of focal onset), and on two occasions progressed to a brief generalised convulsion lasting under a minute without injury. Daytime events have not been reported in the last eight weeks. 

She denies missed doses and uses a pill dispenser with phone reminders. Occupational health notes show good adherence and no concerns at work performance aside from transient post‑ictal tiredness on mornings after nocturnal activity; she has not required rescue medication. Triggers discussed include poor sleep continuity during a recent shift change and increased caffeine intake; she has already reduced caffeine and is keeping a sleep log. There is no intercurrent illness, no alcohol misuse, and pregnancy is not planned. 

We discussed that her epilepsy includes both generalised and focal elements, and that nocturnal clustering may reflect disturbed sleep architecture. She will continue current doses while we gather additional data. I have requested: 

- 72‑hour ambulatory EEG with sleep capture to characterise onset and generalisation. 
- Serum levetiracetam and lamotrigine levels for reference. 
- Updated MRI epilepsy protocol (last imaging 2019). 
- Employer occupational health to continue monthly summaries as a collateral source. 

Safety: She does not drive. Seizure first aid and SUDEP discussions were revisited; her partner has the rescue plan and knows when to call emergency services. She has a written seizure plan shared with occupational health. 

Plan: 
- No change to regular medication today. 
- Continue seizure diary noting time, semiology, and any injuries; attach occupational health monthly summaries. 
- If clusters increase in frequency, duration, or occur with injury, or if single seizures exceed 5 minutes, she will activate her rescue plan. 
- Review in three months, sooner if deterioration. Results will guide whether to adjust lamotrigine (night dose) or consider adjunctive therapy. 

Yours sincerely

demos.evidence_text: He suffers clusters of absence seizures on five days each month
demos.note_text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 February 2020 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy following prior traumatic brain injury; persistent cognitive impairment; osteoporosis; past alcohol dependence with alcoholic hepatitis; currently residing in supported accommodation; smoker; reports social alcohol use and is actively reducing intake. 

Medication: 
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily 
- Tegretol Retard (carbamazepine) 400 mg twice daily 
- Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening 
- Vitamin B compound, Calcichew, Strontium sachets, Thiamine, Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Carbocisteine, Aqueous cream 

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, accompanied by his key worker, who has supported him for the past year. He reports improved sleep routine and better adherence to morning medications with use of a blister pack. He is engaging in light physiotherapy for balance and right-sided stiffness; gait is slow but he mobilises independently with a single-point stick. There have been no recent falls. Appetite is stable and weight is unchanged. He acknowledges occasional social alcohol use at family events but states he is reducing and aiming for abstinence; no binges have been reported by staff. 

Epilepsy history and current status: Bill has focal epilepsy. Seizure pattern has remained largely unchanged since the last clinic visit. He suffers clusters of absence seizures on five days each month. Nocturnal tonic seizures continue to occur around once per year. No daytime convulsions have been recorded recently. He and staff are keeping a simple monthly seizure diary, which aligns with the above pattern. Known triggers include missed morning doses and sleep loss; neither has occurred in the past two months according to care records. No injuries from seizures have been reported. 

Examination: Alert and oriented to person and place; mild executive dysfunction on conversational testing. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Tone mildly increased in right leg with reduced right ankle dorsiflexion; power otherwise antigravity throughout. Sensation grossly intact to light touch. Gait slow with reduced right foot clearance; no ataxia. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams unremarkable. 

Investigations: No new imaging since prior MRI showing left frontal gliosis consistent with old traumatic change. Routine bloods from GP two months ago reportedly normal including LFTs, U&Es, and vitamin D; we will request results for our records. No adverse haematological effects reported on current antiseizure regimen. 

Plan: 
- Continue current antiseizure medications unchanged given stable focal epilepsy pattern. 
- Reinforce adherence and sleep hygiene; continue seizure diary. 
- Request recent blood results from practice (FBC, U&E, LFT, vitamin D); repeat locally if unavailable. 
- Physiotherapy to continue for gait and right leg stiffness; encourage daily walking within supported setting. 
- Support ongoing reduction in social alcohol use; no medication changes on this basis today. 
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if seizure pattern worsens or daytime convulsions occur. 

Yours sincerely

demos.evidence_text: Monthly clusters, typically five or seven seizures over 24 h
demos.note_text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Ahmed  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Ahmed,  

Liam Thompson, DOB: 12-04-1991, Hospital No: NHS8435271 NHS No. 6082194753  
Flat 3B, 19 Market Street, London, W1F 8QA 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Patient reports good daytime tolerability; occasional transient irritability noted during high-stress shifts. Rescue: Buccal midazolam supplied; not yet administered at work.

History since last review: 

He works as a line cook in a commercial kitchen with sustained high heat, sharp implements and heavy pans. He describes Monthly clusters, typically five or seven seizures over 24 h, almost always occurring on late-shift days following consecutive long hours. Events are reported to start with a brief warning of metallic taste and a sense of heat rushing to the head, followed by impaired awareness with automatic stirring or reaching movements; colleagues have witnessed brief periods of unresponsiveness lasting 30–60 seconds. There has been no generalized convulsion reported, and recovery is usually within minutes, though he feels fatigued and slow for several hours afterwards. He notes dehydration and missed meals during service as common antecedents; sleep restriction after double shifts is frequent. No clear intercurrent illness or alcohol trigger. 

Workplace and safety: 

Episodes have occurred at the sauté and grill stations with open flames. He and his manager have implemented a buddy system during late service, immediate step-back from the line at the earliest aura, and relocation to a cool rest area. Knives and hot pans are handed off when a warning is sensed. He wears heat-resistant gloves routinely and avoids high-level ladder access. No injuries to date. He has not required emergency attendance; colleagues time events and ensure supervised rest. 

Plan: 

- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. If the monthly clusters persist at the current pattern, he will keep a precise diary of shift times, sleep duration, hydration, and aura occurrence to guide potential dose optimisation.  
- Maintain ready access to buccal midazolam per existing workplace protocol; training for two additional senior colleagues has been scheduled by his employer.  
- Encourage regular hydration and scheduled carbohydrate intake before peak service; he has arranged short breaks at 18:00 and 20:00 on late shifts.  
- We will obtain updated bloods (U&Es, LFTs) and review irritability symptoms; if troublesome, we can consider mitigation strategies.  
- Provide a brief written workplace risk summary for his manager to reinforce existing precautions. 

Follow-up: 

We will see him again in four months, sooner if cluster frequency increases beyond the described monthly pattern or if any convulsive events occur. 

Yours sincerely
demos.seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per month, 5 to 7 per cluster

demos.evidence_text: He reports that he has been Seizure-free since 13-Nov-2015
demos.note_text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 18 July 2016

Dr A. Patel
St Cuthbert’s Neurology Clinic
Level 3, Outpatients, St Cuthbert’s Hospital, York YO1 7AB

Dear Dr A. Patel,

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-03-1987, Hospital No: H392774 NHS No. 9437710256
Flat 12, 5 Mill Lane, York, YO10 3BG

Diagnoses:

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; aetiology not established. Prior EEG in community reportedly non-diagnostic.

Current Medication:

Lamotrigine 75 mg twice daily

Plan:

Continue current antiepileptic medication. Reinforce sleep hygiene and trigger management. Safety counselling and personalised plan held on record.

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports that he has been Seizure-free since 13-Nov-2015. Prior to that date he described occasional cluster patterns associated with sleep loss, and he has since focused on regular sleep routines and avoiding sleep deprivation. He also confirms that a personalised self-management and safety plan is documented elsewhere in his notes and remains up to date.

On examination today there are no focal neurological deficits. He is tolerating Lamotrigine 75 mg twice daily without adverse effects. Given the current stability and absence of events since November 2015, I have advised continuing the present regimen and maintaining vigilance around recognised triggers, particularly sleep loss. He has a documented plan for what to do should clusters recur, and he understands the escalation steps.

I have not arranged a routine follow-up; however, I am happy to review him sooner if there are any breakthrough events or medication concerns. Otherwise, we can consider review in due course to reassess treatment duration in the context of ongoing stability.

With best wishes,

fields.description: Seizure frequency in Gan label format. One of: '{count} per {unit}' e.g. '2 per week', '1 per 3 month'; '{count} cluster per {unit}, {n} per cluster'; 'unknown'; 'no seizure frequency reference'; or null to abstain. Unit must be singular: day/week/month/year.
Shortest exact contiguous quote from the note supporting the label. Do not include prompt instructions or wrapper text. Null if abstaining.

signature.instructions:
Extract the Gan seizure-frequency label and supporting evidence from a clinical note.

/no_think
Do not use hidden reasoning. Emit only the requested output fields.

The label must match the Gan annotation vocabulary exactly. Abstain (null) only when
the note contains no usable seizure-frequency information.

Synthesis-backed policy:
- Output only canonical Gan labels: N per unit, N to M per unit, N per M unit,
  N cluster per unit, M per cluster, seizure free for N unit, unknown, or
  no seizure frequency reference.
- Use singular units: day, week, month, year.
- Convert daily/nightly to 1 per day, every N unit to 1 per N unit, and
  numeric "or" ranges to "to" ranges.
- Choose the highest current quantified seizure-type frequency.
- Use the full cluster format; do not drop either the cluster period or
  the per-cluster count.
- Use seizure free for N unit only when seizure freedom is at least 6 months.
  If seizure freedom is shorter than 6 months, compute the rate from the
  total events over the described period.
- For year-to-date counts, use months elapsed since January as the denominator.
- Treat "over the last quarter" as an observation window when the note gives
  a per-month rate; do not automatically convert it to per 3 month.
- evidence_text must be an exact contiguous quote from the note.
